channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Yach Report: THR Could Save 100 Million Lives
item.description: Tobacco harm reduction advocate Derek Yach's report proposes that switching 20% of smokers to low-risk nicotine products by 2030 could prevent 100 million premature deaths by 2060. The study forecasts the global growth of oral nicotine users to 80 million by 2030 and emphasizes the potential of synthetic nicotine. Yach highlights faster smoking declines in countries embracing harm reduction policies like Sweden and Japan, contrasting outcomes in prohibition-focused countries like India. The report advocates for quality standards, risk-based taxation, and enhanced access to smoke-free alternatives globally to reduce harm and combat illicit trade. (Published on June 09, 2026bynicotineinsider.com)

item.title: What Estonia Got Wrong About Vaping — and the Lesson the EU Is About to Ignore
item.description: Estonia introduced its own regulations beyond EU requirements, including a national e-liquid tax, a tobacco-only flavor ban, and an online sales ban. These measures led to a significant rise in legal market excise revenue when menthol was re-permitted, indicating a shift away from the black market. Despite the policy changes, Estonia's smoking rate decline started prior to implementation. The data suggests that a flavor ban did not reduce smoking but may have fueled the black market. This case study cautions against overregulation, emphasizing the importance of evidence-based policymaking. Brussels is reminded to consider Estonia's experience as it revises the Tobacco Products Directive. (Published on June 09, 2026bynna.ee)

item.title: Is it ethical to prohibit nicotine products?
item.description: Prohibiting non-combustible nicotine products risks protecting cigarettes rather than public health. When lower-risk alternatives are restricted or banned, adult smokers may be left with fewer realistic options to move away from combustible tobacco. Effective nicotine policy should distinguish between use and harm, prioritising reduced exposure, accurate risk communication and proportionate regulation. (Published on June 09, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: Vaping won't help you escape the 'smoker' tag on insurance. Here's why
item.description: Insurance rules often treat vaping the same as smoking, even when the health risks are not equivalent. In India, insurers may classify anyone using nicotine, including e-cigarettes or e-hookah, as a smoker when assessing health or life cover. This approach raises important questions about whether financial systems are recognising relative risk — or simply grouping all nicotine use into one high-risk category. More accurate risk assessment should reflect the difference between combustible tobacco and lower-risk nicotine alternatives. (Published on June 09, 2026bymoneycontrol.com)

item.title: SA government says illicit tobacco prices increasing in response to store crackdown
item.description: South Australia’s crackdown on illicit tobacco is highlighting the scale and resilience of the illegal cigarette market. Reports of rising black-market prices suggest enforcement may be disrupting supply, but also show how strong consumer demand continues to sustain organised illegal trade. The situation raises wider questions about whether high tobacco taxes, restrictive nicotine policy and limited access to lower-risk alternatives are creating conditions in which illicit markets can thrive. Effective public health policy must reduce smoking-related harm while avoiding approaches that push consumers further into unregulated supply chains. (Published on June 09, 2026byabc.net.au)
